programs.name: Stay NJ
programs.tagline: Up to $6,500 back on property taxes for New Jersey homeowners 65 and older
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and owns their New Jersey home, they can get 50% of their property taxes back — up to $6,500 per year. This means if they pay $10,000 in property taxes, they'll receive $5,000 back.

Stay NJ is designed for older homeowners with annual income below $500,000 who have lived in their home for the full previous year. Because one application (PAS-1) covers multiple New Jersey tax relief programs, the state automatically determines which benefits your parent qualifies for — they may receive Stay NJ plus additional programs like ANCHOR or Senior Freeze for even more savings.
programs.savingsSource: 50% reimbursement of property taxes up to maximum $6,500 benefit

summary: Own and live in NJ home
otherRequirements: Must own home in New Jersey as principal residence
Must have lived in home for all 12 months of the prior year
Mobile homeowners are not eligible
Annual income below $500,000 (based on prior year tax return)
P.I.L.O.T. payments qualify if you're the homeowner

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at propertytaxreliefapp.nj.gov with ID verification — deadline is October 31.

steps.title: Gather your documents
steps.description: Get your latest property tax bill and most recent state income tax return. You'll need a driver's license, state ID, passport card, or passport for identity verification.

steps.title: Apply online
steps.description: Go to propertytaxreliefapp.nj.gov and complete the PAS-1 form. This covers Stay NJ, ANCHOR, and Senior Freeze — the state determines which programs you qualify for.

steps.title: Verify your identity
steps.description: Use ID.me with your government-issued photo ID. Online application is required if you want direct deposit.

steps.title: Submit by deadline
steps.description: Applications must be submitted by October 31 of the tax year you're applying for (October 31, 2026 for 2025 taxes).

applicationGuide.tip: Apply online rather than by mail if you want direct deposit — it's faster and more secure than waiting for a check.

documentsNeeded: Latest property tax bill for your New Jersey home
Most recent New Jersey state income tax return
Government-issued photo ID (driver's license, state ID, passport card, or passport)
Property information from Tax Assessor's office if tax bill unavailable
Proof of full-year residency in the home (utility bills, voter registration, or other official documents)
P.I.L.O.T. payment records if applicable instead of property tax bill

contacts.label: NJ Division of Taxation
contacts.description: Property tax relief programs and Stay NJ questions

contacts.label: New Jersey 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all New Jersey social services

applicationNotes: The October 31 deadline is firm — late applications are not accepted for the tax year
One PAS-1 application automatically checks you for Stay NJ, ANCHOR, and Senior Freeze — you may qualify for multiple programs
Social Security disability alone does not qualify for Stay NJ (unlike some other relief programs) — you must meet the age requirement
Mobile homeowners are specifically excluded even if they pay property taxes or P.I.L.O.T. payments

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for Stay NJ if they moved to their current home in the middle of last year?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must have owned and lived in the home as their principal residence for the full 12 months of the prior year. If they moved in July, they'd need to wait until the following application cycle to meet the full-year requirement.

faqs.question: What if my parent owns a mobile home and pays property taxes on it?
faqs.answer: Mobile homeowners are specifically excluded from Stay NJ, even if they pay property taxes or are assessed like traditional homeowners. This exclusion is explicit in the program rules.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent who has difficulty with online applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can help your parent apply, but the application must be in their name and use their information. You can print the PAS-1 form and mail it to the New Jersey Division of Taxation if online access is difficult, though direct deposit requires online submission.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive if their property taxes are $8,000?
faqs.answer: Your parent would receive $4,000 (50% of $8,000). The reimbursement is exactly half of the property taxes billed, up to a maximum benefit of $6,500 for those paying $13,000 or more in property taxes.

faqs.question: Can my parent get Stay NJ and ANCHOR benefits at the same time?
faqs.answer: The state determines which combination of benefits your parent receives based on their PAS-1 application. Many people qualify for multiple programs simultaneously — Stay NJ, ANCHOR, and Senior Freeze can often be combined for maximum tax relief.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is close to the $500,000 limit?
faqs.answer: The income limit is based on the prior year's state tax return, so use that year's actual income. If your parent's income fluctuates around the limit, apply anyway — the state will make the determination based on the specific tax year's income.

programs.name: Senior Freeze (Property Tax Reimbursement)
programs.tagline: Get reimbursed for property tax increases if your parent is 65+ and has lived in their New Jersey home for years
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and has lived in their New Jersey home since at least 2016, they may get reimbursed for property tax increases above what they paid in their first qualifying year. Income limit: $99,735 or less for 2025 applications (combined for married couples).

This isn't a tax freeze — it's a reimbursement program. Your parent still pays the full property tax bill, but the state reimburses them for increases above their "base year" amount. Because New Jersey administers this through one combined application (PAS-1), your parent can apply for Senior Freeze, ANCHOR, and Stay NJ benefits all at once.

summary: Lived in NJ continuously for 10+ years
otherRequirements: Must have lived in New Jersey continuously for 10 years prior to base year
Must have owned and lived in the same home since base year through application year
Property taxes must be paid by June 1 each year (site fees by December 31 for mobile homes)
Must meet all requirements every year from base year through application year

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through the combined PAS-1 application at nj.gov/treasury/taxation/ptr/ — covers Senior Freeze, ANCHOR, and Stay NJ in one form.

steps.title: Apply online
steps.description: Complete the PAS-1 application at https://www.nj.gov/treasury/taxation/ptr/ or print and mail the forms from the same website.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Submit proof of age/disability, income documentation for relevant years, property tax payment receipts, and proof of continuous New Jersey residency.

steps.title: Submit before deadline
steps.description: 2025 applications due by November 2, 2026. The state will determine your specific benefits and send you a letter.

applicationGuide.tip: The application deadline is strict — 2025 applications must be submitted by November 2, 2026. Don't wait until the last minute.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (Social Security statements or birth certificate)
Social Security disability award letter (if applying based on disability instead of age)
Proof of continuous New Jersey residency for 10+ years
Property deed or home ownership documentation
Property tax payment receipts for base year and current year
Income documentation for all relevant years (tax returns, Social Security statements, pension statements)

contacts.label: NJ Property Tax Relief Helpline
contacts.description: Check application status, order forms, ask questions about Senior Freeze

contacts.label: Automated Status Line
contacts.description: Automated system to check application status

contacts.label: NJ Relay for Deaf/Hard of Hearing
contacts.description: Relay service for hearing-impaired applicants

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for Senior Freeze and ANCHOR at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes — the PAS-1 application covers Senior Freeze, ANCHOR, and Stay NJ all in one form. The state will determine which programs your parent qualifies for and send a letter explaining their benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent moved within New Jersey since 2016?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify if they received Senior Freeze reimbursement for their prior New Jersey residence in the last full year they lived there. Contact (800) 882-6597 to discuss their specific situation.

faqs.question: Does the income limit include both spouses?
faqs.answer: Yes — if your parents are married or in a civil union and living together, their combined income cannot exceed $99,735 for 2025 applications. The limit applies to their total household income.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent missed the eligibility requirements for one year?
faqs.answer: They must meet all requirements every year from their base year through the application year. If they missed even one year, they may lose eligibility and would need to restart with a new base year if they qualify again.

programs.name: Jersey Assistance for Community Caregiving (JACC)
programs.tagline: Helps parents age 60+ who need nursing-level care stay at home when they don't qualify for Medicaid.

localEntities.name: County Aging and Disability Resource Connections via 1-877-222-3737
Examples: Hunterdon Human Services (908-788-1361), Warren Division of Aging (908-475-6591), FCS Monmouth/Mercer/Ocean[2][3][7]

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but earns too much for Medicaid, JACC provides up to $1,156 monthly in home care services. This New Jersey program serves people 60+ who need nursing facility level care but want to stay in their own homes.

JACC covers personal care, homemaking, and care management through a personalized Plan of Care. Because the program includes Participant-Employed Provider (PEP) options, your parent can hire family members or friends as their caregivers. You'll pay a sliding-scale co-pay based on income, but the program covers the majority of costs.

The catch: JACC is specifically for people who don't qualify for Medicaid waiver services like MLTSS. Income limit is 365% of Federal Poverty Level (around $3,300/month for individuals), and assets must be under $40,000 for individuals or $60,000 for couples.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly service cap minus sliding-scale co-pay based on income

summary: Need nursing facility level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be determined clinically eligible for nursing facility level of care (needs daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident
US citizen or Qualified Alien
Living in own home or unlicensed home of relative/friend (not in licensed facility)
Financially ineligible for Medicaid or Medicaid waiver services
No other means available to secure needed services

applicationGuide.summary: Call the NJ EASE helpline at 1-877-222-3737 to start your application and schedule a clinical assessment.

steps.title: Call NJ EASE
steps.description: Call 1-877-222-3737 to connect with your County Aging and Disability Resource Connection. They'll do initial eligibility screening over the phone.

steps.title: Submit documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of income, assets, residency, citizenship, and medical needs. Your county office will tell you exactly what documents they need.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: A care manager will assess whether you meet nursing facility level of care requirements. This determines your eligibility and service needs.

steps.title: Develop Plan of Care
steps.description: If approved, your care manager creates a personalized Plan of Care outlining your specific services within the monthly cap limit.

steps.title: Choose providers
steps.description: Select from approved home care agencies or use the Participant-Employed Provider option to hire family or friends as caregivers.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Subject to funding availability - waitlists may exist
applicationGuide.tip: Ask specifically about current waitlist times in your county when you call - availability varies significantly by region.

documentsNeeded: Social Security card for applicant
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of New Jersey residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Social Security award letter or benefit statement
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with cash values
Medical records documenting need for nursing facility level care
Documentation of current medical expenses and treatments
Proof of citizenship or Qualified Alien status
Income statements from all sources (pensions, retirement accounts)
Health insurance cards and premium payment records

contacts.label: NJ EASE Helpline
contacts.description: Statewide helpline for JACC applications and information

contacts.label: Hunterdon County
contacts.description: Hunterdon Human Services for local JACC administration

contacts.label: Warren County
contacts.description: Warren Division of Aging for local JACC services

applicationNotes: Clinical assessment is required and determines both eligibility and your specific Plan of Care services
Monthly service caps vary slightly by county ($1,090-$1,156) - confirm your county's current limit
Sliding-scale co-pays are billed directly by your service provider, not the state
If you're currently in a licensed facility, you must move to qualifying community housing before services begin
relatedPrograms: Managed Long Term Services and Supports (MLTSS)

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for JACC if they currently receive some Medicaid benefits?
faqs.answer: No, JACC is specifically for people who are financially ineligible for Medicaid or Medicaid waiver services like MLTSS. If your parent qualifies for any Medicaid long-term care services, they cannot receive JACC. However, they may be able to receive JACC for services not covered by their current Medicaid benefits - ask during the assessment.

faqs.question: Can I hire myself as my parent's caregiver through the Participant-Employed Provider option?
faqs.answer: Yes, the PEP option allows your parent to hire family members or friends as their paid caregivers. You'll need to complete provider training and meet program requirements. This option gives families more control over who provides care and when.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income or assets change after they're approved?
faqs.answer: You must report changes in income or assets to your care manager. Significant increases might affect eligibility or co-pay amounts. Your Plan of Care is reviewed periodically, and changes in financial status could impact services.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really, and are there priority categories?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times vary significantly by county and current funding levels. Some areas have immediate openings while others may have several month waits. The program operates on available funding, so ask your county office about current wait times and whether any priority categories apply.

faqs.question: What's the difference between JACC and regular Medicaid home care?
faqs.answer: JACC serves people who earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but still need nursing-level care at home. Unlike Medicaid, JACC has higher income limits (365% FPL vs. Medicaid's much lower limits) and asset limits ($40,000 individual vs. Medicaid's $2,000), but requires co-pays and has monthly service caps.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limits?
faqs.answer: The home you live in is typically not counted toward the $40,000/$60,000 asset limits for JACC eligibility. However, the specific rules about what assets are exempt aren't detailed in available sources, so ask your care manager during the application process about how your parent's home equity affects eligibility.

faqs.question: What specific services does the monthly cap cover, and what doesn't it include?
faqs.answer: The $1,090-$1,156 monthly cap covers services in your personalized Plan of Care, such as personal care assistance, homemaking, and care management. Medical care, prescription drugs, and equipment typically aren't covered - those remain your responsibility through Medicare or other insurance.

faqs.question: Can we apply while my parent is still in the hospital or rehab facility?
faqs.answer: You can start the application process while your parent is in a facility, but JACC services can only begin once they're living in qualifying community housing (their own home or unlicensed home of family/friend). Discharge planners may be able to help coordinate the transition and application timing.

programs.name: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged & Disabled (PAAD)
programs.tagline: If your parent lives in New Jersey and earns less than $54,943/year, they may qualify for $5-7 prescription copays instead of full drug prices.

localEntities.name: Central processing in Trenton; pharmacies statewide accept PAAD

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and lives in New Jersey, PAAD can dramatically reduce their prescription costs. Instead of paying full price for medications, they'll pay just $5 per generic drug and $7 per brand-name drug — PAAD covers the rest. This applies to all FDA-approved prescription medications, insulin, and insulin supplies.

Income limit for 2026: $54,943 or less if single, $62,390 or less if married. Because there are no asset limits, your parent can qualify even if they own their home or have savings. The catch: if they're Medicare-eligible, they must enroll in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan, and PAAD will pay the premium for qualifying low-cost plans.

Because PAAD works alongside Medicare Part D rather than replacing it, your parent gets the broad coverage of Medicare's formulary with the low copays of a state assistance program. One team at the state level manages both the prescription benefits and Part D premium payments.
programs.savingsSource: Estimated based on $5-7 copays versus typical Medicare Part D copays of $15-50+ per prescription for common medications

summary: Age 65+ or receiving Social Security disability
Must enroll in Medicare Part D if Medicare-eligible
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident
Not eligible for Medicaid
Must enroll in Medicare Part D if Medicare-eligible
U.S. citizenship not required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at NJSaves.org or call 866-657-2835 for assistance — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Check if you need Medicare Part D first
steps.description: If your parent has Medicare, they must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan or Medicare Advantage plan with Part D coverage before applying. Call 1-800-792-9745 for help choosing a qualifying low-premium plan.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income (tax returns or Social Security statements), proof of New Jersey residency, Medicare cards if applicable, and proof of Social Security disability if under 65.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at NJSaves.org (click 'Start New Application') or mail the AP-2 form to NJ Department of Human Services, PAAD/Lifeline/Senior Gold, PO Box 715, Trenton, NJ 08625-0715.

steps.title: Wait for approval and card
steps.description: Once approved, your parent will receive a PAAD card to use at pharmacies statewide. The state will also handle Medicare Part D premium payments for qualifying plans.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is Medicare-eligible, don't skip the Part D enrollment step — PAAD won't cover medications without it, and the state can help you choose a qualifying low-premium plan.

documentsNeeded: Proof of income from all sources (tax returns, Social Security award letters, pension statements)
Proof of New Jersey residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Medicare card (Parts A, B, and D if applicable)
Proof of Social Security Title II Disability benefits if under age 65
Documentation showing you're not eligible for Medicaid
Social Security card
Birth certificate or other proof of age
Green card or other proof of lawful permanent residency if not a U.S. citizen
Current prescription list from your doctor or pharmacy
Medicare Part D plan information if already enrolled

contacts.label: NJ Division of Aging Services
contacts.description: PAAD application help and general program questions

contacts.label: Medicare Part D Plan Information
contacts.description: Help choosing qualifying Medicare Part D plans for PAAD

contacts.label: NJ 2-1-1
contacts.description: General assistance with all New Jersey social services

applicationNotes: PAAD only pays Medicare Part D premiums for specific low-cost plans at or near the regional benchmark — enrolling in a higher-premium plan on your own may disqualify you from premium assistance
Income limits are updated annually based on federal guidelines, so use the current year's figures when applying
If your parent's medication isn't covered by their Medicare Part D plan's formulary, you'll need to get an exception through Part D first — PAAD can't override formulary restrictions
PAAD is often confused with Senior Gold, which has higher income limits but different copay amounts — make sure you're applying for the right program
relatedPrograms: Senior Gold (higher income limits, different copays)
New Jersey Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for both PAAD and Senior Gold?
faqs.answer: No, your parent can only be enrolled in one program at a time. PAAD has lower income limits ($54,943 single/$62,390 married) with $5 generic/$7 brand copays. Senior Gold has higher income limits but different copay structures. Apply for PAAD first if income-eligible since the copays are lower.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's Medicare Part D plan has a high premium?
faqs.answer: PAAD only pays premiums for plans at or near the regional benchmark (typically the lowest-cost plans in your area). If your parent chooses a more expensive plan, they may lose premium assistance but can still get the $5/$7 copays. Call 1-800-792-9745 to find qualifying low-premium plans before enrolling.

faqs.question: Does PAAD cover diabetic test strips and supplies?
faqs.answer: No, PAAD does not cover diabetic testing supplies or most supplies covered by Medicare Part B. It covers FDA-approved prescription drugs, insulin, insulin supplies, and syringes/needles for multiple sclerosis injectables only. Your parent would need to rely on Medicare Part B or other insurance for test strips.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help complete the application, especially if your parent has difficulty with paperwork or technology. You can apply online at NJSaves.org or complete the AP-2 form by mail. You'll need access to all their financial and medical documentation.

faqs.question: What if my parent's medication isn't covered by their Medicare Part D plan?
faqs.answer: PAAD cannot override Medicare Part D formulary restrictions. If a medication isn't covered, you must first request an exception or prior authorization through the Medicare Part D plan. If approved by Part D, then PAAD will provide the $5/$7 copay. Call your Part D plan directly for exception requests.

faqs.question: Will owning a house or having savings disqualify my parent?
faqs.answer: No, PAAD has no asset limits. Your parent can own their home, have bank accounts, investments, or other assets and still qualify. Only income matters for eligibility — $54,943 or less for singles, $62,390 or less for married couples in 2026.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent moves from New Jersey to another state?
faqs.answer: PAAD benefits end when your parent is no longer a New Jersey resident. They would need to apply for prescription assistance programs in their new state. Make sure to notify PAAD of the address change and ask about the effective date of termination.

faqs.question: Can my parent get PAAD if they have Medicaid or are applying for it?
faqs.answer: No, PAAD eligibility specifically excludes people who have Medicaid. If your parent is considering both programs, compare the prescription benefits — Medicaid typically has very low copays already. If they lose Medicaid eligibility later, they can then apply for PAAD.

programs.name: Lifeline Credit
programs.tagline: Up to $225/year off your parent's electric and gas bills if they're 65+ and qualify for prescription assistance.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and already gets New Jersey's prescription assistance (PAAD) or receives SSI, they qualify for up to $225/year off their electric and natural gas bills. The Lifeline Credit appears automatically on their utility bill — no monthly paperwork or vouchers to manage.

For 2026, your parent qualifies if their annual gross income is below $54,943 (single) or $62,390 (married). They must be a residential electric or gas customer — the program doesn't cover oil, propane, or other heating sources. Only one person per household can receive the credit.

SSI recipients get this benefit automatically in their monthly check and don't need to apply separately. Everyone else applies through the same NJSave application used for prescription assistance, making it a simple add-on to programs your parent may already use.
programs.savingsSource: Annual bill credit for eligible residential electric and/or natural gas customers

summary: Must qualify for PAAD or receive certain benefits
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident
Enrolled in or eligible for Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged and Disabled (PAAD), Medical Assistance to the Aged (MAA), Medical Assistance Only (MAO), New Jersey Care, or receiving SSI/SSDI
Residential electric or natural gas customer (excludes oil, propane, wood, coal)
Only one applicant per household
U.S. citizenship not required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through NJSave or wait for the automatic mailing in August if you already receive certain benefits.

steps.title: Check if you need to apply
steps.description: SSI recipients get Lifeline Credit automatically in their monthly check — no application needed. If you receive MAA, MAO, or New Jersey Care, applications are mailed automatically each August.

steps.title: Apply online through NJSave
steps.description: Visit the official NJ Department of Human Services website and complete the NJSave application. The Lifeline questions are included when you apply for PAAD prescription assistance.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income from the past 30 days, Social Security cards, photo ID, proof of address, and your most recent utility bill.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the online application or mail the form if you received it automatically. Contact your utility company (PSE&G, NJNG) or call NJ Department of Human Services Division of Aging Services if you need help.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent already receives prescription assistance through PAAD, they may automatically qualify for Lifeline Credit without a separate application.

documentsNeeded: Proof of income for past 30 days (Social Security award letter, pension statements, pay stubs)
Social Security cards for all household members
Valid photo ID (driver's license, passport, or government-issued ID)
Proof of New Jersey address (rental lease, mortgage statement, property tax bill, or deed)
Most recent electric utility bill
Most recent natural gas utility bill
Documentation of current benefit enrollment (PAAD card, SSI award letter, or Medicaid card if applicable)

contacts.label: NJ Division of Aging Services
contacts.description: Primary program administrator for Lifeline Credit applications and questions

contacts.label: PSE&G Customer Service
contacts.description: For questions about Lifeline Credit on PSE&G bills

contacts.label: New Jersey Natural Gas
contacts.description: For questions about Lifeline Credit on NJNG bills

applicationNotes: SSI recipients do not apply separately — the benefit is automatically included in their monthly SSI payment
Applications are automatically mailed each August to current MAA, MAO, and New Jersey Care beneficiaries
Eligibility is checked as of July 1st or within the next 6 months of application
Only natural gas and electric utilities are covered — oil, propane, wood, and coal heating systems don't qualify
relatedPrograms: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged and Disabled (PAAD)

faqs.question: Can my parent get Lifeline Credit if they heat with oil or propane?
faqs.answer: No. Lifeline Credit only applies to residential electric and natural gas customers. Homes heated with oil, propane, wood, or coal don't qualify for this program. Look into LIHEAP energy assistance instead, which covers all heating sources.

faqs.question: What if both my parents live together — can they both apply?
faqs.answer: No. Only one person per household can receive Lifeline Credit, even if both parents meet the age and income requirements. The application must be in the name of the person who pays the utility bills or their spouse.

faqs.question: My parent gets SSI — do they still need to apply for Lifeline Credit?
faqs.answer: No. SSI recipients get the Lifeline Credit automatically as part of their monthly SSI payment. They should not submit a separate application for this program.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both PAAD prescription assistance and Lifeline Credit at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes. The NJSave application covers both programs. When you apply for PAAD, the Lifeline Credit questions are included in the same application, making it a simple one-stop process.

faqs.question: How does the credit appear on the utility bill?
faqs.answer: The annual credit of up to $225 appears directly on your parent's electric and/or natural gas bill. There are no vouchers to redeem or separate payments to manage — the utility company applies the credit automatically.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income goes up after they're approved?
faqs.answer: Eligibility is typically checked annually as of July 1st. If your parent's income rises above the limits ($54,943 for single, $62,390 for married), they'll need to report the change and may lose eligibility for the following year.

faqs.question: My parent already gets the automatic application in the mail — do they still need to reapply each year?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even though MAA, MAO, and New Jersey Care beneficiaries receive applications automatically each August, they must complete and return the form annually to continue receiving the Lifeline Credit.

faqs.question: Can my parent get Lifeline Credit if they're not a U.S. citizen?
faqs.answer: Yes. U.S. citizenship is not required for Lifeline Credit. Your parent only needs to be a New Jersey resident who meets the age, income, and utility customer requirements.

programs.name: New Jersey Statewide Respite Care Program
programs.tagline: If you're caring for a disabled parent in New Jersey, get paid companion care, overnight help, or adult day services to give you a break.
programs.intro:
If you're providing daily care for a parent with a chronic disability or illness in New Jersey, you may qualify for companion care, overnight help, adult day services, or even short-term facility placement to give you respite. Because this program covers in-home care, nursing services, and temporary placement options, your parent can get the supervision they need while you take a break or handle other responsibilities.

Your parent must receive daily care from you (unpaid) and have functional impairments certified by their doctor. Income limit: $2,982/month for a single person, $5,964/month for married couples in 2026. Asset limit: $40,000 for single, $60,000 for couples. You'll pay a sliding scale co-pay of 0-25% based on income — families at the lowest income levels pay nothing.
programs.savingsSource: Sliding scale service with 0-25% co-pay based on income

countedAssets: Liquid assets convertible to cash within 20 working days

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must have chronic physical or mental disability requiring daily supervision or assistance with basic needs, certified by a licensed medical provider
otherRequirements: Must live in New Jersey community (not in assisted living or nursing home)
Must receive daily care from unpaid caregiver age 18+
Cannot currently participate in MLTSS, JACC, Alzheimer's Adult Day Services, or Congregate Housing Services

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Aging and Disability Resource Connection at 1-877-222-3737 to apply — no forms needed to start.

steps.title: Call the state helpline
steps.description: Contact Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) at 1-877-222-3737 to begin your application and get connected to your county's sponsor agency.

steps.title: Complete eligibility screening
steps.description: Provide income, asset, and household information to determine if you meet financial guidelines. Only the care recipient's income counts, not the caregiver's.

steps.title: Get medical certification
steps.description: Have your parent's doctor complete documentation certifying their functional impairments and need for daily supervision or assistance.

steps.title: Meet with county agency
steps.description: Work with your county's local sponsor agency to complete the full application and discuss service options available in your area.

applicationGuide.tip: Priority goes to families where the disabled person is at risk of nursing home placement due to caregiver burnout — mention this if it applies to your situation.

documentsNeeded: Valid photo ID for care recipient
Social Security card for care recipient (and spouse if married)
Proof of New Jersey residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter or pension statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Documentation of any other income sources
Investment or asset documentation if applicable
Medical certification of functional impairments from licensed provider
Documentation showing care recipient lives in community (not facility)
Proof of current caregiver arrangement (unpaid family or friend care)

contacts.label: Aging and Disability Resource Connection
contacts.description: Primary application line for all New Jersey aging services

contacts.label: New Jersey 2-1-1
contacts.description: 24/7 helpline for all social services and local resources

applicationNotes: Each county has its own sponsor agency that administers the program locally — service availability and wait times may vary by county
You can switch from JACC or the Alzheimer's Adult Day Services Program to Statewide Respite, but you cannot participate in both simultaneously
The Caregiver Directed Option allows reimbursement for services or items that make caregiving easier — ask about this flexibility
Emergency or planned respite is available once per year after enrollment, which can be valuable for urgent situations

faqs.question: Does my income count toward eligibility if I'm the caregiver living with my parent?
faqs.answer: No, only your parent's income counts toward the eligibility limits. Even if you live in the same household, your income is irrelevant for this program. The limits are $2,982/month for a single person or $5,964/month for married couples in 2026.

faqs.question: Can my parent qualify if they're already getting some paid help?
faqs.answer: No, your parent must be receiving daily care from an unpaid caregiver to qualify. If they're currently paying for regular care services, they won't meet the eligibility requirements for this program.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot qualify while living in assisted living, nursing homes, or any residential care facility. This program is only for people living in community settings like their own home or apartment.

faqs.question: How much will we actually pay for services?
faqs.answer: You'll pay between 0-25% of the service cost based on your parent's income. Families at the lowest income levels pay nothing, while those closer to the income limits pay up to 25% of costs.

faqs.question: Can we apply if my parent is already on Medicaid?
faqs.answer: It depends on which Medicaid program. You cannot participate in MLTSS, JACC, Alzheimer's Adult Day Services, or Congregate Housing Services simultaneously. However, you may be able to switch from JACC or the Alzheimer's program to Statewide Respite.

faqs.question: What happens if our county has no availability?
faqs.answer: Service availability varies by county since each has its own sponsor agency. If your county has wait times, ask about emergency respite options or whether nearby counties have faster availability through the ADRC at 1-877-222-3737.

faqs.question: Do we need to requalify every year?
faqs.answer: The program likely requires periodic reviews to confirm continued eligibility and assess changing needs, but specific requalification timing isn't detailed in available sources. Your county sponsor agency will explain their review schedule during enrollment.

faqs.question: Can we get help with medical equipment or home modifications?
faqs.answer: The program includes a Caregiver Directed Option that allows reimbursement for services or items that make caregiving easier, which may include some equipment. Ask your county sponsor agency specifically about what items qualify under this option.

programs.name: Alzheimer's Adult Day Services Program
programs.tagline: Subsidized dementia-specialized day care for your parent while you work or get respite
programs.intro:
If your parent has Alzheimer's or another form of dementia, New Jersey will help pay for specialized adult day care services. This program subsidizes attendance at participating day centers that have high staff-to-client ratios and activities designed specifically for cognitive loss. Your parent can attend at any stage of their dementia as long as family or friends provide daily assistance at home.

Because these centers specialize in dementia care, your parent will be in an environment designed for their needs rather than a general senior center. The state covers most costs through income-based subsidies, though you'll pay a copay based on your parent's income. Centers offer both social model programs (activities and supervision) and medical model programs (nursing care and therapy services).

summary: Not currently on Medicaid or MLTSS
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Your parent must require daily assistance from family or friends due to functional impairments caused by their dementia
otherRequirements: Must have medical diagnosis of qualifying dementia (Alzheimer's, Pick's disease, dementia due to Parkinson's, Huntington's, multi-infarct/vascular dementia, Creutzfeldt-Jakob, Fronto-Temporal, Lewy body, dementia due to normal pressure hydrocephalus, or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome)
Cannot currently participate in Medicaid (NJ FamilyCare, MLTSS), JACC, or Congregate Housing Services Program

applicationGuide.summary: Find a participating center near you and contact them directly — they'll handle the subsidy application and income verification.

steps.title: Find participating centers
steps.description: Visit https://www.nj.gov/humanservices/ to find dementia-specialized adult day centers in your area that accept program subsidies.

steps.title: Contact centers directly
steps.description: Call the centers you're interested in to discuss your parent's needs, tour the facility, and ask about current availability and copay amounts.

steps.title: Submit application through chosen center
steps.description: The center will help you complete the subsidy application and gather required documentation for income and asset verification.

steps.title: Get medical certification
steps.description: Your parent's doctor must provide written diagnosis confirmation of qualifying dementia and functional impairments requiring care.

applicationGuide.tip: Contact multiple centers since availability and copay amounts may vary — some may have immediate openings while others have waiting lists.

documentsNeeded: Proof of New Jersey residency (utility bill, lease, or driver's license)
Medical diagnosis documentation from licensed provider confirming qualifying dementia type
Written certification of functional impairments requiring daily assistance
Social Security award letters for all household income sources
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements if applicable)
Life insurance policies with face values
Vehicle titles and registration
Documentation showing you're not currently receiving Medicaid, JACC, or Congregate Housing Services

contacts.label: Division of Aging Services
contacts.description: Single point of access for program information and applications

contacts.label: NJ 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and program referrals

applicationNotes: Income and asset limits aren't published in program brochures — contact the Division of Aging Services or participating centers for current financial thresholds
You must use centers that specifically participate in this program and specialize in dementia care — not all adult day centers qualify
Even with subsidies, you'll still pay an income-based copay — ask centers for specific amounts during your initial contact
The program has been operating since 1987 specifically to provide family caregiver relief through affordable specialized care
relatedPrograms: NJ Medicaid Long Term Services and Supports (MLTSS)

faqs.question: Can my parent participate if they're already getting some Medicaid services?
faqs.answer: No, your parent cannot participate if they're currently receiving Medicaid (NJ FamilyCare), Managed Long Term Services and Supports (MLTSS), JACC, or Congregate Housing Services Program. You'll need to choose between this program and those other benefits.

faqs.question: What's the difference between social model and medical model centers?
faqs.answer: Social model centers focus on activities, supervision, and social interaction for people with dementia. Medical model centers include nursing care, therapy services, and more intensive medical oversight. Both types participate in this program and specialize in dementia care.

faqs.question: How much will I actually pay with the income-based copay?
faqs.answer: Copay amounts aren't published in program materials and vary based on your parent's income and assets. Contact participating centers directly for specific copay calculations based on your parent's financial situation.

faqs.question: Does my parent's dementia need to be at a certain stage to qualify?
faqs.answer: No, your parent can participate at any stage of dementia as long as they have a medical diagnosis of qualifying dementia and require daily assistance from family or friends. The program serves people from early-stage through advanced dementia.

faqs.question: Can I apply to multiple adult day centers at once?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it's recommended since availability varies by location. Contact several participating centers to compare services, copay amounts, and availability. Each center will help you with the same subsidy application process.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your copay amount may change since it's based on current income and assets. Contact your center or the Division of Aging Services to report income changes and get your copay amount recalculated.

programs.name: Senior Citizen E-ZPass Discount
programs.tagline: If your parent drives and has an E-ZPass, they can get 10% off tolls on the NJ Turnpike and Garden State Parkway after age 65.

localEntities.name: Delaware River Port Authority (DRPA) for bridges

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and drives on New Jersey toll roads, this discount can cut their toll costs by 10% during off-peak hours on the NJ Turnpike and Garden State Parkway. Because there are no income or asset limits, any senior with a valid driver's license and vehicle registration can qualify — regardless of how much money they have.

The catch: your parent must be actively driving the vehicle (not just a passenger), must have a New Jersey E-ZPass account specifically, and the discount only applies during off-peak hours (weekdays off-peak and weekends). There's also a separate program for four Delaware River bridges that provides an $18 credit if your parent makes 18+ trips in a month.

Because the program requires annual renewal tied to vehicle registration dates, some enrollment periods may be less than 12 months. Your parent can only have one discounted E-ZPass tag, and if anyone else uses it, they'll lose the discount permanently.

otherRequirements: Must have an active New Jersey E-ZPass account
Must have a valid driver's license from any state
Must have a passenger vehicle registered in your name or spouse's name
Must be the driver in the vehicle at time of toll transaction
Only one E-ZPass tag per senior (must start with 009 or 022)
Annual renewal required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at seniordiscount.njta.gov with your NJ E-ZPass account number and driver's license; processing time not specified.

steps.title: Get a New Jersey E-ZPass account
steps.description: If you don't have one, apply at www.ezpassnj.com or call 1-888-288-6865. You need a NJ account specifically — out-of-state E-ZPass won't work.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need your driver's license, vehicle registration, NJ E-ZPass account number (13 digits), and E-ZPass tag number (11 digits).

steps.title: Apply for NJTA Senior Discount
steps.description: Go to https://seniordiscount.njta.gov to apply online, or call 732-750-5300 to enroll by phone.

steps.title: Apply for DRPA bridge discount separately
steps.description: If you use Benjamin Franklin, Walt Whitman, Betsy Ross, or Commodore Barry bridges, call 856-968-2000 press 5 or 215-218-3750 press 5 for the separate DRPA Senior Plan.

applicationGuide.tip: You need separate applications for the Turnpike/Parkway discount and the bridge discount — they're run by different authorities.

documentsNeeded: Copy of valid driver's license (to prove age 65+ — no other ID accepted)
Copy of passenger vehicle registration in your name or spouse's name
NJ E-ZPass account number (13 digits)
E-ZPass tag/transponder number (11 digits)
Top portion of lease agreement if vehicle is leased (showing you as lessee)

contacts.label: NJTA Senior Discount Enrollment
contacts.description: Enroll in Turnpike and Garden State Parkway senior discount

contacts.label: New NJ E-ZPass Account
contacts.description: Open a new New Jersey E-ZPass account

contacts.label: DRPA Bridge Senior Discount
contacts.description: Press 5 for senior discount on four Delaware River bridges

applicationNotes: You must have a New Jersey E-ZPass account — accounts from other states won't work for this discount
Annual renewal is required and timed to your vehicle registration renewal date, which may be less than 12 months from enrollment
Only one discounted tag per senior — if family members use your tag, you'll permanently lose discount privileges
The discount only applies during off-peak hours — no savings during rush hour periods
relatedPrograms: NJ Property Tax Deduction for Seniors

faqs.question: Can my parent use this discount if they don't live in New Jersey?
faqs.answer: Yes, there's no residency requirement. Your parent just needs a New Jersey E-ZPass account, which out-of-state residents can open. Vehicle registration can be from any state for Turnpike/Parkway, or NJ/PA/DE for DRPA bridges.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent lets other family members use their discounted E-ZPass tag?
faqs.answer: They'll permanently lose their discount privileges. The program is tag-specific and only the enrolled senior can use it. Each senior can only have one discounted tag.

faqs.question: Does this work during rush hour when tolls are highest?
faqs.answer: No, the discount only applies during off-peak hours — weekdays off-peak and weekends. You won't save money during peak rush hour periods when tolls are most expensive.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both the Turnpike discount and the bridge discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they're separate programs requiring separate applications. Call 732-750-5300 for Turnpike/Parkway and 856-968-2000 press 5 for the four Delaware River bridges.

faqs.question: What if my parent's E-ZPass account is from New York or Pennsylvania?
faqs.answer: They'll need to open a New Jersey E-ZPass account specifically. Out-of-state E-ZPass accounts don't qualify for this discount program.

faqs.question: How much can my parent actually save with this discount?
faqs.answer: Savings depend on how often they drive toll roads during off-peak hours. The 10% discount applies to their off-peak tolls on Turnpike and Parkway only. For bridges, they get $18 credit only if they make 18+ trips per month.

stateOverview.intro:
New Jersey offers 8 senior programs that could save your parent hundreds to thousands of dollars annually. These range from property tax relief (up to $6,500 back through Stay NJ) to prescription drug assistance ($5-7 copays instead of full prices) to respite care while you work. Most programs target specific needs — housing costs, medical expenses, or caregiver support — rather than general financial assistance.

Start by checking if your parent qualifies for property tax relief or prescription assistance, as these typically offer the largest savings. Then look into caregiver support programs if you're providing daily care. Each program has different income limits and requirements, so your parent may qualify for some but not others.

startHere.name: Stay NJ
startHere.why: Up to $6,500 back on property taxes — the largest potential benefit in New Jersey.

startHere.name: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged & Disabled (PAAD)
startHere.why: Reduces prescription costs to $5-7 per medication, saving hundreds monthly for parents with multiple prescriptions.

startHere.name: Senior Freeze (Property Tax Reimbursement)
startHere.why: Protects against property tax increases for long-term homeowners, providing ongoing savings.

startHere.name: Jersey Assistance for Community Caregiving (JACC)
startHere.why: Helps parents who need nursing-level care stay at home when income is too high for Medicaid.

byNeed.description: Two programs that can reduce or reimburse property taxes for long-term New Jersey homeowners.

programs: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged & Disabled (PAAD)
byNeed.description: Reduces prescription copays to $5-7 for New Jersey seniors under income limits.

programs: New Jersey Statewide Respite Care Program
byNeed.description: Help paying for home care or getting respite when you're the primary caregiver.
Subsidized adult day care specifically designed for parents with dementia.
Small but automatic savings on electric, gas, and toll costs for qualifying seniors.

quickFacts: New Jersey's property tax programs (Stay NJ and Senior Freeze) offer the largest potential savings but require homeownership and residency history.
PAAD prescription assistance has an income limit of $54,943/year for a single person and requires New Jersey residency.
Several programs are linked — if your parent qualifies for PAAD, they automatically qualify for Lifeline Credit utility assistance.
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Most New Jersey senior programs are qualification-based benefits requiring specific income limits, age requirements, or care needs. For example, PAAD requires income under $54,943/year, while JACC requires nursing-level care assessment. Only one program (Senior Freeze) functions as a general resource with broader eligibility. Unlike some states that offer universal information services, New Jersey focuses on targeted financial assistance for those who meet specific criteria.
